summary: DeVault argues that Google has adopted Microsoft's infamous 'embrace, extend, extinguish' strategy against open standards across multiple products. He traces this pattern through Gmail's treatment of email federation, AMP's co-opting of the open web, the abandonment of XMPP in chat products, and the killing of Google Reader and RSS. He acknowledges Google's open source contributions but criticizes their poor collaboration with external contributors on key projects like Chromium and Android. The post concludes with a call to diversify the internet and resist Google's growing monoculture.
key_points: Gmail extends email with proprietary features like self-destructing messages while its aggressive spam filter punishes independent mail servers
AMP masquerades as a standard but serves primarily to keep users on Google's platform, punishing non-participating sites in search rankings
Google systematically abandoned open federated protocols (XMPP, RSS) after gaining dominant market positions through them
Google's open source projects are open in name but hostile to external contribution, functioning more as published code than collaborative projects
The pattern is consistent across products: adopt open standards to gain users, add proprietary extensions, then marginalize the original standard
money_quotes: Embrace, extend, extinguish is a core part of Google's playbook today.
AMP is perhaps the most blatant of Google's strategies, serving only to further Google's agenda at the expense of everyone else.
Sure, they make a lot of software open source, but they don't collaborate with anyone.
Google emails a link to a webpage with the actual email content, and does magic in their client to make it look seamless. Thus, they 'extend' email.
The 'extinguish' with GMail is also well underway - it's infamous for having an extremely strict spam filter for incoming emails from people who run personal or niche mail servers.
It's important that we work to diversify the internet and fight the monoculture they're fostering.
Google writes papers about AI, presumably to incentivize their academics with recognition for their work. This is great until you notice that the crucial piece, the trained models, is always absent.
key_insight: Google has systematically adopted Microsoft's embrace-extend-extinguish playbook across email, web, chat, and syndication, leveraging open standards to gain dominance before replacing them with proprietary alternatives.
